verdict_reasoning: BAX is a pro-apoptotic Bcl-2 family member that resides as a soluble monomer in the cytosol of healthy cells and, upon apoptotic stimulation, translocates to the mitochondrial outer membrane where it oligomerizes and forms pores to release cytochrome c. Its entire functional lifecycle is intracellular (cytosol ↔ MOM, with retrotranslocation by Bcl-xL). There is no documented stable display of the BAX protein body on the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane — no cell-surface antibody, CAR-T, or ADC programs target surface BAX; therapeutic modalities (BAX activators/inhibitors) are cell-penetrant small molecules engaging the cytosolic/MOM pool. No germline, cancer ecto-form, cell-death-externalization, or shedding biology is established for BAX itself.
